name: Gunra Ransomware File Encryption (.ENCRT Extension)
description: Detects mass file creation or renaming with the .ENCRT extension, which is the file extension used by Gunra ransomware to mark encrypted files. This rule triggers when a non-system process creates multiple .ENCRT files in rapid succession, indicating active ransomware encryption. Detection is technique-focused and will also catch other ransomware families reusing this extension.
note:
## Triage
- Identify the initiating process and its parent.
- Check if the process is a known legitimate encryption tool.
- Investigate the scope: how many files were affected and in which directories.
- Correlate with shadow copy deletion or recovery inhibition events.
- If confirmed malicious, immediately isolate the host.
false_positives: Legitimate encryption software that uses .ENCRT extension (extremely unlikely)
Backup applications that rename files during processing
query:
sequence by host.name, process.entity_id with maxspan=1m
  [file where event.action in ("creation", "rename") and file.extension == "ENCRT"] with runs=5

name: Gunra Ransom Note Creation (R3ADM3.txt)
description: Detects creation of the Gunra-specific ransom note file R3ADM3.txt. This filename is unique to the Gunra ransomware family and its presence on disk is a high-confidence indicator of an active or completed ransomware attack. Any creation of this file should be treated as a critical security incident.
note:
## Triage
- This is a high-fidelity alert with very low false positive rate.
- Immediately isolate the affected host from the network.
- Identify the process that created the file and trace its execution chain.
- Check for .ENCRT files in the same and adjacent directories.
- Initiate incident response procedures for ransomware.
false_positives: F0RT1KA security test execution (verify with SOC team)
Extremely unlikely in production environments

name: Shadow Copy Deletion via vssadmin
description: Detects execution of vssadmin.exe with arguments to delete Volume Shadow Copies. This is a critical ransomware technique (T1490) used by Gunra, Conti, REvil, LockBit, and virtually all modern ransomware families to prevent file recovery from VSS snapshots. The detection is technique-generic and not specific to any single ransomware strain.
note:
## Triage
- Determine if the vssadmin execution was part of legitimate IT maintenance.
- Check the parent process: ransomware-spawned vssadmin will have unusual parents.
- Look for correlated file encryption activity or ransom note creation.
- If unauthorized, immediately isolate the host and check for remaining shadow copies.
false_positives: IT administrators performing scheduled VSS maintenance
Disk space management scripts that resize shadow storage
query:
process where event.action == "start" and
  process.name == "vssadmin.exe" and
  process.args : ("delete", "shadows")

name: Shadow Copy Deletion via WMIC
description: Detects execution of wmic.exe to delete Volume Shadow Copies via WMI. This is a common alternative to vssadmin for shadow copy deletion used by many ransomware families including Gunra. Real-world attackers frequently use WMIC because it can be scripted more easily and may bypass some endpoint controls that only monitor vssadmin.
note:
## Triage
- WMIC-based shadow copy deletion is almost never legitimate.
- Check the parent process for the execution chain.
- Correlate with file encryption events and ransom note drops.
- Immediately isolate the host if this is confirmed unauthorized.
false_positives: Extremely rare in legitimate operations
Automated IT scripts using WMI for VSS management (should be baselined)
query:
process where event.action == "start" and
  process.name == "wmic.exe" and
  process.args : ("shadowcopy", "delete")

name: Boot Recovery Disabled via BCDEdit
description: Detects bcdedit.exe being used to disable Windows recovery options. Ransomware families including Gunra use this technique to prevent victims from recovering via Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE) or Safe Mode. This makes the impact of encryption more severe by eliminating OS-level recovery paths.
note:
## Triage
- BCDEdit recovery disabling is extremely uncommon in normal operations.
- Determine the parent process and user context.
- Check if this correlates with other ransomware indicators (VSS deletion, file encryption).
- If unauthorized, the system boot configuration may need manual restoration.
false_positives: System administrators configuring boot options during maintenance windows
OEM deployment scripts modifying boot configuration
query:
process where event.action == "start" and
  process.name == "bcdedit.exe" and
  process.args : ("recoveryenabled", "no")

name: Rapid Mass File Rename Indicating Ransomware Encryption
description: Detects a single process performing mass file rename or creation operations in rapid succession, which is a hallmark behavior of ransomware encryption. This rule is generic and will detect any ransomware that renames files during encryption, not just Gunra. The threshold of 20 file operations within 30 seconds from a single process is designed to minimize false positives while catching active encryption.
note:
## Triage
- Identify the process performing the mass file operations.
- Check file extensions being applied -- known ransomware extensions increase confidence.
- Determine the scope: which directories are affected.
- If the process is unknown or suspicious, immediately isolate the host.
- Check for associated ransom note creation or shadow copy deletion.
false_positives: Backup software performing file operations
Archive extraction tools (7zip, WinRAR) extracting large archives
File migration or synchronization utilities
Automated build systems creating many output files
query:
sequence by host.name, process.entity_id with maxspan=30s
  [file where event.action in ("creation", "rename", "modification")] with runs=20

name: Rapid System Information Discovery Commands
description: Detects rapid execution of multiple system information discovery commands (hostname, whoami, systeminfo, ipconfig, net) from a single parent process within a short time window. Gunra and other ransomware families perform system reconnaissance before beginning encryption to determine the target environment and tailor their attack. This burst pattern of discovery commands is suspicious when originating from a non-administrative tool.
note:
## Triage
- This is a medium-confidence detection that requires context.
- Check if the parent process is a legitimate IT management or inventory tool.
- Look for follow-on activity: file encryption, shadow copy deletion, lateral movement.
- If the parent process is unknown, investigate its origin and signing status.
false_positives: IT inventory and asset management scripts
System monitoring agents performing health checks
Administrator troubleshooting sessions
query:
sequence by host.name, process.parent.entity_id with maxspan=2m
  [process where event.action == "start" and process.name in ("hostname.exe", "whoami.exe", "systeminfo.exe", "ipconfig.exe", "net.exe")] with runs=3

name: Ransomware Kill Chain - Encryption with Recovery Inhibition
description: High-confidence correlation rule that detects the combination of shadow copy deletion followed by mass file operations, which represents a complete ransomware kill chain. This two-stage pattern (recovery inhibition then encryption) is used by virtually all modern ransomware including Gunra, LockBit, Conti, and BlackCat. The combination of these two behaviors within a 10-minute window on the same host is extremely unlikely to be legitimate.
note:
## Triage
- This is a HIGH-CONFIDENCE alert combining multiple ransomware indicators.
- Immediately isolate the affected host from the network.
- Begin incident response procedures for active ransomware.
- Determine if shadow copies were actually deleted (vssadmin list shadows).
- Assess the scope of file encryption.
- Check for lateral movement to other hosts.
false_positives: Extremely unlikely - this combination of behaviors is almost exclusively malicious
query:
sequence by host.name with maxspan=10m
  [process where event.action == "start" and
    ((process.name == "vssadmin.exe" and process.args : "delete") or
     (process.name == "wmic.exe" and process.args : "shadowcopy" and process.args : "delete") or
     (process.name == "bcdedit.exe" and process.args : "recoveryenabled"))]
  [file where event.action in ("creation", "rename") and file.extension == "ENCRT"]

name: Generic Ransomware File Extension Detection
description: Detects creation of files with known ransomware extensions including .ENCRT (Gunra), .encrypted, .locked, .crypted, .locky, .cerber, .ryuk, .conti, .lockbit, .phobos, .dharma, .djvu, and others. This broad detection covers many ransomware families and variants. The rule requires multiple files with ransomware extensions from a single process to reduce false positives.
note:
## Triage
- Identify which ransomware extension was detected.
- Check the initiating process and its reputation.
- Determine the number of files affected.
- If a known ransomware extension, begin IR procedures immediately.
false_positives: Legitimate encryption tools that use common extension names
File recovery testing tools
Security product testing
query:
sequence by host.name, process.entity_id with maxspan=2m
  [file where event.action in ("creation", "rename") and
    file.extension in ("ENCRT", "encrypted", "locked", "crypted", "enc", "crypt", "locky", "cerber", "ryuk", "conti", "lockbit", "phobos", "dharma", "djvu", "stop")] with runs=3

name: Ransom Note Content Pattern Detection
description: Detects creation of text files in user directories that match common ransom note naming patterns. Gunra uses R3ADM3.txt, but this rule also covers common ransom note filenames used by other families (README.txt in non-standard locations, DECRYPT*.txt, RECOVER*.txt, etc.). Files matching these patterns in user document directories are strong indicators of ransomware activity.
note:
## Triage
- Read the file content to determine which ransomware family is involved.
- Check for encrypted files in the same directory.
- Identify the process that created the ransom note.
- Begin incident response procedures if confirmed.
false_positives: Legitimate README.txt files (filter by directory context)
Security awareness testing materials
